title: Postmortem Changes -- Issues of Concern -- Classification of Post Mortem Changes
content: Circulation of blood is a continuous process carried out by the pumping action of the heart in a living individual. However, once the person dies, the circulation comes to a halt, and the blood starts moving towards the dependant regions of the body due to gravity. This effect results in reddish-blue staining of those low-lying dependent regions of the body, known as the livor mortis, post mortem staining, post mortem lividity, or post mortem hypostasis. [25] [26] [27] During the initial phases, patches of discoloration start appearing in the dependent regions in 1 to 3 hours after death. These increase in size and spread all over the dependent regions in 4 to 6 hours and are fully developed within 6 to 8 hours. So, in case of the body of an individual lying on the floor of a room, the back of the individual will show postmortem staining.

title: Coronary Artery Dissection (Archived) -- History and Physical
content: Patients with spontaneous coronary artery dissection usually present with symptoms and signs characteristic of acute myocardial infarction (MI). Chest or shoulder pain, syncope, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and nausea associated with elevation of cardiac enzymes are the most common presentation. A small proportion (3% to 14%) of patients present with resuscitated ventricular arrhythmias. Some cases present as sudden unexplained death, although, because of the challenges of accurate post-mortem diagnosis, this condition is under-represented in most cases. A high index of clinical suspicion is required for women presenting with typical chest pain and ECG or cardiac biomarker abnormalities.

title: Posterior Myocardial Ischemia (Archived) -- Prognosis
content: Patients with inferior or lateral MI with posterior extension are at an increased risk of complications including death, likely due to the size of the infarct. [16] [24] Further, isolated PMI is less likely to be detected on ECG, owing to the absence of traditional or familiar “red-flag” ECG findings of AMI (for example, ST elevations). [3] [11] Patients who present with STEMI and undergo PCI in under 2 hours have 30-day mortality of 3% to 5%. [25] [26] NSTEMI with proven coronary occlusion (OMI) on coronary angiography has double the mortality rate at 30 days (2% to 5%) compared to NSTEMI without a demonstrated coronary occlusion (NOMI). [27] [28] [29] [30]  There are no specific data to support a significant difference in mortality of posterior STEMI compared to STEMI of other anatomical location.

title: Postinfarction Ventricular Septal Rupture -- Differential Diagnosis
content: Distinguishing on clinical grounds between acute mitral regurgitation and rupture of the ventricular septum in patients with STEMI in whom a loud systolic murmur suddenly develops may be difficult. Such differentiation can be made most readily by color flow Doppler echocardiography. Also, right heart catheterization with a balloon-tipped catheter can readily distinguish between these 2 complications. Patients with VSR demonstrate a step-up in oxygen saturation in blood samples from the right ventricle and pulmonary artery as compared with those from the right atrium. Patients with acute mitral regurgitation lack this step-up and may demonstrate tall C-V waves in both the pulmonary capillary and pulmonary arterial pressure tracings. [9] [10]

title: Posterior Myocardial Ischemia (Archived) -- History and Physical
content: The physical exam is not always a reliable indicator of the presence of ACS. Patients with actively infarcted myocardium may range from mild discomfort to obtundation. Frequently, the episode of discomfort that prompts an encounter for ACS may have resolved by the time of presentation. Vital signs may range from hypertension to hypotension in the case of AMI induced cardiogenic shock with a compensatory tachycardia. However, bradycardia is also a common vital sign abnormality if the sinoatrial (SA) node is involved (most commonly in the setting of a proximal occlusion of a dominant RCA). The presence of an S3 gallop, crackles, and cold extremities may indicate acute heart failure and cardiogenic shock as a result of AMI. A new murmur may indicate papillary muscle rupture or new valve dysfunction. The patient may have a clenched fist or open hand pressed against the sternum, termed Levine sign and Cossio sign respectively. [7]

title: Anteroseptal Myocardial Infarction -- History and Physical
content: Auscultation: Precordium on four cardiac areas for acute onset gallops, murmurs, or rubs. Acute ischemia may cause an S4, a paradoxical S2 heart sound splitting, or a new systolic murmur at the mitral area which may occur due to mitral regurgitation resulting from papillary muscle dysfunction. S3 may be present when there is decompensated heart failure. These signs are however non-specific as they may occur without an MI. The presence of a precordial rub may help identify acute pericarditis and may help exclude acute MI. A pleural friction rub may be audible in pneumonitis and/or pleuritis and may support a non-MI cause of chest pain.

title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2399
content: Acute myocardial infarction, in its many clinical presentations—including ST‑segment elevation infarctions caused by occlusion of the left anterior descending artery, its septal or proximal branches, the intermediate artery, the mid‑portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending or posterior lateral branches of the right coronary artery, as well as non‑ST‑segment elevation infarctions, widespread infarctions, and those occurring during procedures or postoperatively—always involves injury to the myocardium. Regardless of the specific coronary vessel or segment that is blocked, the pathological finding site for each of these myocardial infarction subtypes is the myocardial tissue itself, underscoring that the heart muscle is the central organ affected across the full spectrum of acute infarct presentations. This unified understanding emphasizes that diagnostic evaluation, therapeutic interventions, and patient monitoring should focus on the extent and location of myocardial damage, irrespective of the particular coronary artery involved or the clinical context of the infarction.

title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2197
content: Acute infarct is a cardiac pathology that can manifest with a wide range of morphologic patterns. It is associated with acute Q wave infarction – including the widespread form – and with acute Q wave myocardial infarction. Various forms of acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction are also linked to acute infarct, such as the general disorder and specific presentations caused by occlusion of distinct coronary segments: the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the mid portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and right coronary artery occlusion overall. In addition, acute infarct encompasses acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, and acute myocardial infarction that occurs during a medical procedure. Non‑Q wave morphologies are included as well, with both acute non‑Q wave infarction and its widespread variant being associated with acute infarct. Furthermore, acute infarct is linked to acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction and to subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction, reflecting its relevance to both initial and later ischemic events. Together, these associations illustrate that acute infarct can present with diverse electrocardiographic and anatomic features, emphasizing the importance of recognizing its various morphologies for accurate diagnosis and management.

title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2819
content: Acute myocardial infarction (احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ) is closely related and may be considered synonymous with coronary artery occlusion (انسداد الشريان التاجي). It is also mapped to coronary artery embolism (انصمام الشريان التاجي), indicating that these terms describe similar pathological events in which a blockage of a coronary artery—whether by occlusion or embolic material—leads to sudden injury of the heart muscle. Recognizing the interchangeability of these concepts assists clinicians in diagnosing and managing the acute coronary syndrome that results from compromised coronary blood flow.

title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
content: Acute myocardial infarction encompasses a spectrum of disorders that all involve injury to the myocardium. Specific forms include Acute Q‑wave infarction – widespread, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), and Acute STEMI due to occlusion of distinct coronary branches such as the left coronary artery, the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the diagonal branch of the LAD, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and the septal branch of the LAD. Additional variants are Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to circumflex branch occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, and Acute widespread myocardial infarction. Despite the differing anatomic sites of arterial blockage and electrocardiographic patterns (Q‑wave versus ST‑segment elevation), each of these entities shares the common finding site of the myocardium, underscoring that the primary pathological substrate in all these conditions is myocardial tissue damage. Recognizing the specific coronary artery involved helps guide reperfusion strategies and risk stratification, while the universal involvement of the myocardium highlights the need for prompt diagnosis and treatment to preserve cardiac function.

title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2405
content: Acute Q wave infarction – widespread, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (including the generic disorder and the specific forms caused by occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the mid‑portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and involvement of the left anterior descending coronary artery), acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), acute widespread myocardial infarction, postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction are all distinct clinical entities that share a common anatomic focus: the myocardium. Each of these conditions represents an acute loss of blood supply to myocardial tissue, whether the injury is precipitated by specific coronary artery branch occlusions, surgical or procedural contexts, or broader patterns of infarction. Recognizing that all of these varied presentations localize to the myocardium underscores the central role of myocardial injury in the pathophysiology, diagnosis, and management of acute coronary syndromes.

title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1870
content: Acute myocardial infarction, in its many clinical presentations, is consistently localized to the myocardium (myokard). Whether described as an acute Q‑wave infarction (including widespread forms), an acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction caused by occlusion of the left coronary artery, the right coronary artery, the proximal left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, the septal branch of the anterior descending branch, or any combination of these arterial territories, the pathological finding site remains the myocardial tissue. This also applies to acute non‑Q‑wave infarctions, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions (including widespread and subsequent anterior variants), and myocardial infarctions that occur during procedures. Recognizing that all these diverse infarct types share the myocardium as the affected organ underscores the central role of myocardial injury in the diagnosis, monitoring, and treatment of acute coronary syndromes.

content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with heart murmurs (نفخة قلبية). The presence of a heart murmur can serve as an important clinical indicator of underlying coronary artery pathology, reflecting turbulent blood flow that may result from atherosclerotic narrowing or ischemic changes in the coronary vessels.
